exemplar.title: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: The essay maintains a clear focus on the prescribed title, repeatedly returning to the key terms âacquiring knowledgeâ and âsense of wonder.â
Two areas of knowledge (natural sciences and human sciences) are explicitly identified and used throughout, providing a coherent comparative structure.
Arguments in each AOK are supported by real-world examples (e.g., stellar scintillation, multiverse theory, Piagetâs stages), demonstrating an ability to ground claims in concrete cases.
There is an awareness of alternative perspectives, noting that knowledge can both diminish and amplify wonder depending on the knowerâs purpose and methodological differences.
The discussion is organized and coherent, with recognizable linkages between examples and the central question.
weaknesses: The treatment of examples is often descriptive and lacks deeper critical analysis of their epistemic status or wider implications.
Key TOK concepts (ways of knowing, justification, evidence quality) are underutilized, limiting the depth of the knowledge-questions exploration.
Several arguments rely on repetition of broad claims (e.g., âdepends on the knowerâs purposeâ) without sufficient nuance or specific real-life scenarios.
The essay does not formulate an explicit knowledge question early on, which weakens the overall TOK framing.
Analysis of counterclaims and methodological contrasts remains brief and tends to state rather than evaluate alternative viewpoints.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Based on the strand assessments, the essay aligns most closely with the 5â6 band: it sustains a focus on the title, links to two areas of knowledge, and supports arguments with examples, demonstrating some awareness of different points of view. However, analysis remains largely descriptive, examples are not fully critically explored, and core TOK terminology and knowledge-question framing are underdeveloped. Awarding 6 marks reflects the essayâs clear structure and effective use of examples while recognizing its limited depth and evaluative sophistication required for higher bands.

annotations.comment: The paragraph on Piagetâs theory is incomplete and ends abruptly. Ensure each example is fully developed and linked back to how it affects the evolution of wonder in human learners.
The citation style in footnote 5 shifts between APA and MLA formats. Adopt a consistent citation style throughout to meet IB formatting guidelines.
The description of Piagetâs stages is accurate but lacks critical evaluation. Examine potential cultural biases or limitations in Piagetâs framework to demonstrate deeper critical thinking.
The statement on naturalistic methodology is too vague. Elaborate with a concrete example of naturalistic observation to show how such methods both constrain and provoke wonder.

The recurrent claim that wonder shifts based on the knowerâs purpose is underexplored. Provide a real-life scenario showing how different motivations lead to divergent reactions to the same knowledge.

The essay does not reference any specific ways of knowing (e.g., reason, emotion, imagination). Integrate WOK analysis to demonstrate deeper TOK engagement and show how different WOKs influence wonder.
The counterpoint about expertise generating new questions is promising but unsupported. Cite specific research or theorists to substantiate the claim that advanced knowledge leads to renewed wonder.

The introduction lacks a clearly articulated knowledge question to guide the inquiry. The student should explicitly state a TOK knowledge question early on to focus the analysis and help readers track the line of reasoning.

Definitions of âacquiring knowledgeâ and âsense of wonderâ are presented descriptively rather than critically. The student should connect these terms to TOK concepts (e.g., ways of knowing, knowledge frameworks) to deepen understanding.
The student clearly identifies two areas of knowledge (natural sciences and human sciences) in the introduction, establishing a coherent structure for the comparative discussion that follows.
Sentence structures in the introduction are overly long and complex, which hinders clarity. Consider splitting lengthy sentences into shorter ones to improve readability and coherence.
The essay uses the pronoun âheâ to describe the knower, which may exclude other perspectives. Adopt gender-neutral language (e.g., âtheyâ) to ensure inclusivity throughout.
The description of the scientific method is accurate but too general. The student should link these methods directly to how they affect a knowerâs sense of wonder in specific scientific contexts.

The example of star twinkling and atmospheric refraction is relevant, but the analysis remains descriptive. Explore how this shift from mystery to explanation impacts the knowerâs emotional engagement with the natural world.
Footnotes 1 and 2 duplicate the same URL. Ensure each citation is unique and accurately reflects the source being referenced.
The citation for the white paper lacks publisher details and access date. Include full bibliographic information in line with IB referencing conventions.

The introduction of multiverse theory is a strong example of knowledge enhancing wonder. To deepen analysis, evaluate its epistemic status and discuss why empirical uncertainty surrounding the theory fuels curiosity.

This discussion of knowledge shaping worldviews lacks explicit TOK terminology (e.g., âjustification,â âperspectiveâ). Integrate IB vocabulary to align the analysis with assessment expectations.
The contrast between natural and human sciences is noted but underdeveloped. Compare methodologies more deeply to show how objective versus subjective practices impact the knowerâs sense of wonder.
The Minnesota Twin Study is a relevant RLS, but the interpretation is overly deterministic. Address geneâenvironment interactions or alternative views to present a balanced evaluation.
The Embryo Project Encyclopedia is a secondary source. Strengthen the human sciences discussion by incorporating primary research articles where possible.
This summary paragraph largely reiterates earlier points without synthesizing implications. The student should draw clear conclusions about the overall impact of knowledge on wonder.
The conclusion restates the thesis but lacks a final reflective insight. Suggest ending with an open question or consideration of how these findings might inform future TOK inquiries.
The closing reflection on methodological differences is a strength. To deepen the analysis, discuss how other ways of knowing, such as imagination, play a role in sustaining a sense of wonder.
Several sentences exhibit minor grammatical issues, such as subjectâverb agreement. Review these for clarity and correctness to enhance the overall professionalism of the essay.
The paragraph on Piagetâs theory is incomplete and ends abruptly. Ensure each example is fully elaborated and avoids unfinished sentences.
The use of first-person (âI disagreeâ) departs from formal TOK essay style. Adopt an impersonal academic voice to maintain an objective tone.
There is no exploration of the ethical dimension of acquiring knowledge. Consider discussing moral implications of knowledge that alters a sense of wonder.
Footnote formatting is inconsistent across pages. Ensure uniform formatting of sources according to a chosen style guide (e.g., IB TOK referencing).
The discussion of reliability in natural versus human sciences is pertinent but underdeveloped. Analyze how different levels of certainty influence emotional engagement and wonder.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
